description: Concurrent use significantly increases bleeding risk through complementary anticoagulant mechanisms.
alternative: Use Paracetamol for pain relief instead of Aspirin.
mechanism: Pharmacodynamic synergy — both inhibit clotting cascade

description: NSAIDs potentiate anticoagulant effect of Warfarin and damage gastric mucosa causing serious GI bleeding.
alternative: Use Paracetamol. Monitor INR closely if NSAID unavoidable.
mechanism: CYP2C9 inhibition increases Warfarin plasma levels

description: Combination increases risk of lactic acidosis — rare but potentially fatal metabolic complication.
alternative: Avoid alcohol completely while on Metformin.
mechanism: Both inhibit hepatic gluconeogenesis, increasing lactate

description: Amiodarone inhibits CYP3A4 metabolism of Simvastatin causing dangerous accumulation and rhabdomyolysis.
alternative: Switch to Rosuvastatin or Pravastatin (less CYP3A4 dependent).
mechanism: CYP3A4 inhibition by Amiodarone increases statin AUC by 75%

description: Amiodarone increases Digoxin levels by 70–100% risking toxicity: nausea, arrhythmias, visual disturbances.
alternative: Reduce Digoxin dose by 50% and monitor serum levels closely.
mechanism: P-glycoprotein inhibition reduces Digoxin renal clearance

description: Ciprofloxacin inhibits Warfarin metabolism dramatically increasing bleeding risk.
alternative: Use alternative antibiotic if possible. Monitor INR daily.
mechanism: CYP1A2 inhibition raises Warfarin (R-enantiomer) levels

description: Risk of serotonin syndrome — potentially life-threatening. Both increase serotonin activity.
alternative: Avoid combination. Use non-serotonergic analgesics like Paracetamol.
mechanism: Combined serotonergic effect exceeds safe threshold

description: High risk of serotonin syndrome. Symptoms: agitation, confusion, rapid heart rate, high blood pressure.
alternative: Switch to non-SSRI or use different analgesic entirely.
mechanism: Fluoxetine also inhibits CYP2D6, impairing Tramadol metabolism

description: NSAIDs reduce renal clearance of Methotrexate leading to toxic accumulation.
alternative: Avoid all NSAIDs with Methotrexate. Use Paracetamol cautiously.
mechanism: NSAID-mediated reduction in renal prostaglandins decreases MTX clearance

description: Erythromycin inhibits Carbamazepine metabolism causing toxicity: diplopia, ataxia, drowsiness.
alternative: Use Azithromycin instead — significantly less CYP3A4 inhibition.
mechanism: CYP3A4 inhibition raises Carbamazepine levels 2–3 fold

description: Clarithromycin strongly inhibits CYP3A4 and CYP2C9 causing marked rise in Warfarin effect.
alternative: Use Azithromycin. If Clarithromycin necessary, reduce Warfarin dose and monitor INR daily.
mechanism: Dual CYP inhibition — most dangerous macrolide-Warfarin combination

description: Both slow heart rate — combination causes dangerous bradycardia and potential heart block.
alternative: Avoid combination. Use Amlodipine (dihydropyridine CCB) instead of Verapamil.
mechanism: Additive AV node suppression through complementary mechanisms

description: Ibuprofen blocks Aspirin binding to COX-1, reducing its antiplatelet effect and cardiovascular protection.
alternative: Take Aspirin 2 hours before Ibuprofen or use Paracetamol for pain.
mechanism: Competitive COX-1 binding — Ibuprofen occupies active site first

description: Amlodipine increases Atorvastatin exposure by ~18%. Monitor for muscle pain (myalgia).
alternative: Cap Atorvastatin dose at 40mg. Switch to Rosuvastatin if symptoms occur.
mechanism: Mild CYP3A4 inhibition by Amlodipine

description: Furosemide may increase Metformin plasma levels. Risk of lactic acidosis in dehydration states.
alternative: Monitor renal function and hydration status closely.
mechanism: Furosemide reduces renal tubular secretion of Metformin

description: Combination causes dangerous hyperkalemia (high potassium) — can cause fatal arrhythmias.
alternative: Monitor serum potassium regularly. Avoid potassium supplements.
mechanism: Both retain potassium through different mechanisms — additive effect

description: Omeprazole inhibits CYP2C19 slowing Diazepam metabolism and increasing sedation risk.
alternative: Reduce Diazepam dose. Consider Pantoprazole as alternative PPI.
mechanism: CYP2C19 inhibition increases Diazepam half-life significantly

description: Amlodipine inhibits Simvastatin metabolism increasing myopathy and rhabdomyolysis risk.
alternative: Do not exceed 20mg Simvastatin with Amlodipine. Switch to Rosuvastatin.
mechanism: CYP3A4 inhibition by Amlodipine raises Simvastatin AUC

description: Combination greatly increases risk of peptic ulcers and gastrointestinal bleeding.
alternative: Add Omeprazole for gastric protection. Use Paracetamol for pain where possible.
mechanism: Corticosteroids impair mucosal defence; NSAIDs inhibit prostaglandin synthesis

description: PPIs raise gastric pH reducing absorption of Levothyroxine and may cause hypothyroidism.
alternative: Take Levothyroxine 30–60 min before Omeprazole on empty stomach.
mechanism: pH-dependent dissolution — Levothyroxine requires acidic environment for absorption

description: Additive CNS depression with increased risk of respiratory depression — especially in elderly.
alternative: Use lowest effective doses. Monitor respiratory rate and sedation level.
mechanism: Synergistic CNS depression through different receptor mechanisms

description: Combination significantly increases risk of maculopapular skin rash (up to 20% incidence).
alternative: Use Cephalexin or Azithromycin instead of Amoxicillin if possible.
mechanism: Mechanism unclear — possibly immune-mediated hypersensitivity reaction

description: Furosemide causes potassium and magnesium loss which sensitises heart to Digoxin toxicity.
alternative: Monitor electrolytes regularly. Supplement potassium or add Spironolactone.
mechanism: Hypokalemia increases binding of Digoxin to myocardial Na/K-ATPase

description: Omeprazole inhibits CYP2C19 activation of Clopidogrel reducing its antiplatelet effect by ~40%.
alternative: Switch to Pantoprazole or Rabeprazole — less CYP2C19 inhibition.
mechanism: Clopidogrel is a prodrug requiring CYP2C19 activation to active thiol metabolite

description: Ciprofloxacin may cause both hypo and hyperglycaemia unpredictably in diabetic patients.
alternative: Monitor blood glucose frequently. Consider alternative antibiotic.
mechanism: Ciprofloxacin stimulates insulin secretion and reduces hepatic glucose output

description: Minor pharmacokinetic interaction — generally well tolerated at standard doses.
alternative: No significant action needed. Routine monitoring.
mechanism: Minor competition for plasma protein binding

description: Additive sedative effect. May cause drowsiness — avoid driving or operating machinery.
alternative: Take Cetirizine at night. Warn patient about sedation.
mechanism: Additive CNS depression through different receptor pathways

description: Calcium channel blockers may slightly impair insulin secretion and glucose tolerance.
alternative: Monitor blood glucose. Usually not clinically significant at standard doses.
mechanism: CCBs block calcium-dependent insulin secretion from pancreatic beta cells

description: High or prolonged doses of Paracetamol (>2g/day) can increase INR slightly.
alternative: Limit Paracetamol to 2g/day maximum in patients on Warfarin. Monitor INR weekly.
mechanism: Paracetamol metabolite inhibits Vitamin K epoxide reductase at high doses

description: NSAIDs may reduce antihypertensive effect of ACE inhibitors by blocking prostaglandin synthesis.
alternative: Monitor blood pressure. Use Paracetamol for pain where possible.
mechanism: NSAIDs reduce renal prostaglandins that mediate ACE inhibitor vasodilation

description: Beta-blockers may mask tachycardia — an early warning sign of hypoglycaemia.
alternative: Monitor for other hypoglycaemia signs (sweating, hunger). Patient education important.
mechanism: Beta-blockade masks adrenergic hypoglycaemia symptoms
